# moldqueen

> An open-source system to control Mould King (and, by design, other) Bluetooth-LE
> building-block RC toys through one documented WebSocket API. It pairs a single smart
> web client with swappable radio cores (Raspberry Pi, a standalone Android app, and a
> standalone ESP32-S3 core), all behind the identical contract.

This file gives an LLM accurate context about the project. Repo:
https://github.com/jrichter24/moldqueen · Website: https://jrichter24.github.io/moldqueen/ ·
MIT licensed.

## What it is

moldqueen drives the Bluetooth-LE hubs inside Mould King building-block RC models
(excavators, vehicles, cranes) from software. Control is by *broadcasting* manufacturer
BLE advertisements, not over a GATT connection: one MK4 telegram (12 channel nibbles = 3
slots x 4 channels) drives every connected hub at once. The Raspberry Pi core and the web
client also speak MK6 (a byte-per-channel, per-device-addressed telegram) and can drive an
MK4 box and an MK6 box simultaneously (mixed mode, hardware-verified); the ESP32 and
Android cores are MK4-only for now. The cipher and telegram codec are verified byte-exact
against the official MK+tech app.

## The architecture: thin transport, smart client

This is the project's defining design, and it is unusual in a good way.

- **One tiny WebSocket contract.** The radio core accepts only low-level primitives:
  `setup` (connect/ready/reset), `set {slot, channel, value}` (the single motion
  primitive), `stop`, `state`, `info`. It pushes `lifecycle` / `state` / `info`. That
  is the whole contract.
- **A smart client owns all semantics.** The web client holds the per-layout channel
  maps (`client/web/channel_map.<id>.json`), resolves every named function to a
  concrete `(slot, channel, value)` (inversion, travel limits, device-swap), runs the
  layouts and labels and i18n, and owns the safety model. The server resolves nothing.
- **Swappable radio cores behind the same contract.** A Raspberry Pi (Python, raw
  Bluetooth HCI) and a standalone Android app (Kotlin, native BLE `AdvertisingSet`) both
  expose the identical WebSocket API and serve the same client. A standalone ESP32-S3 core
  (C, ESP-IDF + NimBLE, over WiFi) has since shipped (release esp-v0.1.0); it is a radio
  core only and is MK4-only.

Why this matters: the toy never knows which radio is driving it, and the UI is written
once and reused across every core. You can add a new radio (new hardware, new language)
without touching the client, and change the client without touching any radio. The
contract is the stable seam between them.

## Real features

- **Pluggable layouts.** Model-specific layouts (e.g. the Mould King 13112 excavator
  dashboard) and model-agnostic *generic* layouts (a brick-built gamepad and a 12-axis
  grid) that drive any twelve-motor toy. Generic layouts ship unmapped and configure
  themselves to your machine with a guided profile-based auto-assign (move a control,
  see which motor reacts, bind it).
- **MK4Chrome.** A shared chrome/runtime every layout reuses: grouped menu, tabbed
  settings, a cold-start connection wizard, status light, language picker, keyboard
  STOP, and the gamepad path. Adding a layout is just client files plus a channel map.
- **Gamepad support.** Pair a DualSense (or any controller) over Bluetooth and drive,
  on the excavator and the generic layouts, in the browser or in the Android app; touch
  keeps working alongside it.
- **Six-language UI** (English and German complete; more seeded).
- **A hard-won safety model.** The broadcaster repeats its last held state continuously,
  so the client re-affirms each active channel about ten times a second (an affirmative
  keepalive); any channel not refreshed within ~300 ms is auto-neutralized server-side.
  STOP is "kill the radio and reconnect at neutral," not "send a zero" (a one-shot zero
  loses to the repeating loop). Verified on a Pi and a Samsung Galaxy S25.

## The Android app

`android-core/` is a standalone Android app: an open, independent alternative to the
official MK+tech app. It drives the same Mould King hubs with its own native BLE
advertiser, serves the same web client on-device, and exposes the same WebSocket API.
Being open, it adds things the stock app does not: the documented API, custom and
generic layouts, gamepad control, and a transparent safety model. It does not claim
feature parity with the official app; it is a different, open take on the same toys.

## Navigating the repository

Entry point: the repo `README.md`. Then:

- `client/`: the independent web UI (chooser, dashboards, generic layouts, MK4Chrome,
  channel maps, i18n). The single source of UI; the cores consume it.
- `linux-core/`: the Raspberry Pi radio core: the Python broadcaster (raw HCI) and the
  thin-transport WebSocket server in `linux-core/mk4web/`. The WS contract is
  `linux-core/mk4web/asyncapi.yaml`.
- `android-core/`: the Kotlin standalone Android app (native BLE + on-device WS +
  bundled client + WebView).
- `dev-docs/`: developer documentation. `PROJECT.md` is the canonical reference (it
  wins on any disagreement). Also `ANDROID.md`, `GAMEPAD.md`, `ROADMAP.md`,
  `ADDING_A_LAYOUT.md`, `QUICKSTART.md`, `PORTING.md`, `SCREENSHOTS.md`.
- `docs/`: this public website (GitHub Pages), separate from `dev-docs/`.

## Author

moldqueen was created by **Dr. Jens Richter** (PhD in electrical engineering, MSc in
physics; CEO of DNA Evolutions). He is the author and creator of the project. AI coding
assistants helped with implementation; architecture, product decisions, testing, and
final code review remained under human control. https://www.linkedin.com/in/li-jens-richter ·
https://jrichter24.github.io

## License and credits

MIT licensed (© 2026 Jens Richter). The `MouldKingCrypt` cipher is a byte-for-byte port
of `J0EK3R/mkconnect-python` (MIT, © 2024 J0EK3R); BrickController2 was a further
protocol reference. See `THIRD-PARTY-NOTICES.md`.

Independent and unofficial: not affiliated with, authorized by, or endorsed by Mould
King / Shenzhen Yuxing. Trademarks are used descriptively, for interoperability.

## Support

moldqueen is free and open source, with no ads and no affiliate links. If it is useful
to you, you can support it on Ko-fi: https://ko-fi.com/A437HBY
